questions.question: What does research show about the duration of nature exposure needed to produce measurable stress reduction?
options: At least 8 hours of wilderness immersion
Even 20-minute nature exposure produces measurable cortisol reduction and stress relief
Only multi-day wilderness trips provide benefits
Nature exposure has no measurable effect on stress
questions.explanation: Harvard research and multiple studies document that even brief nature exposure — as little as 20 minutes — produces measurable reductions in cortisol (stress hormone) and self-reported stress. You don't need wilderness or long durations; proximity and frequency matter more than remoteness.

questions.question: According to Attention Restoration Theory, what four components make nature experiences maximally restorative?
options: Competition, productivity, achievement, and performance
Fascination (effortless engagement), Extent (rich environment), Being Away (psychological distance), and Compatibility (personal preference match)
Speed, intensity, difficulty, and danger
Cost, exclusivity, duration, and wilderness remoteness
questions.explanation: Attention Restoration Theory (Kaplan & Kaplan) identifies four components: Fascination (stimuli that capture attention effortlessly), Extent (environment large/coherent enough to feel immersed), Being Away (psychological escape from demands), and Compatibility (environment matches what you personally find restorative). These maximize cognitive restoration.

questions.question: What does horticultural therapy research show about gardening for mental health?
options: Gardening has no mental health benefits
Meta-analysis of 17 studies shows significant reductions in depression (SMD −0.61) and anxiety (SMD −0.55) from horticultural therapy interventions
Only professional farmers benefit from gardening
Gardening is harmful for mental health
questions.explanation: A 2025 meta-analysis of 17 studies found horticultural therapy produces significant reductions in depression (standardized mean difference −0.61) and anxiety (SMD −0.55). Structured gardening programs are effective in psychiatric settings and accessible for people with limited mobility (container gardening, raised beds).

questions.question: What does evidence show about urban green spaces (city parks) versus remote wilderness for mental health benefits?
options: Only remote wilderness provides mental health benefits; city parks are useless
Even small urban green spaces provide measurable stress reduction and attention restoration; proximity and frequency matter more than wilderness remoteness
You must travel to national parks to experience any nature benefits
Urban nature and wilderness provide identical benefits in all situations
questions.explanation: Research shows even urban green spaces (city parks, tree-lined streets, botanical gardens) provide measurable stress reduction and attention restoration. You don't need wilderness access. Proximity and frequency matter more than remoteness — a 20-minute park visit provides real benefits. Urban nature is accessible and effective.

questions.question: How should nature-based recreation be positioned relative to clinical mental health treatment?
options: Nature replaces all medication and psychotherapy
Nature-based recreation is a valuable adjunct to comprehensive mental health care (alongside therapy, medication if needed, and social support) — not a replacement for clinical treatment
Nature has no role in mental health care
Only people without mental illness should spend time in nature
questions.explanation: Nature-based recreation is a valuable mental health support practice and evidence-supported adjunct to comprehensive care — but it does NOT replace medication, psychotherapy, or crisis intervention when clinically needed. Best use: nature as part of comprehensive mental health care, not as sole treatment for moderate-to-severe conditions.
